title: A simple and versatile method for frequent 24 h blood sample collection in healthy older adults
author: Akintola, Jansen, et al.
materials: No validated guidelines were available for older subjects.

steps.title: Standard protocols
steps.instruction: According to the European guidelines [2], the age range for blood donation is 18–65 years. Above the age of 65 years, blood donation is allowed only at the discretion of the responsible physician [2]. This medical discretion can be applied on an individual basis or through a systematic approach based on an appropriate risk assessment.

steps.title: Standard protocols
steps.instruction: Based on the European guideline [2] the maximum amount of blood that can be withdrawn is dependent on the weight and sex of the person, to a maximum of 500 ml over a 24 h period. No more than 15% of the estimated blood volume is to be collected as whole blood, because of the risk of adverse reactions [2].

steps.title: Standard protocols
steps.instruction: Standard venepuncture and phlebotomy guidelines involve visual identification of forearm veins (median cubittal and median veins) followed by venepuncture [4], [5].

steps.title: Standard protocols
steps.instruction: Inspection of the antecubital fossa in preparation for cannulation, skin preparation with 2% chlorhexidine and insertion of appropriate cannula. Pressure is applied in cases of failed cannulation.

steps.title: Standard protocols
steps.instruction: No published validated protocol was found for older adults. A previous study recommended addition of heparin 100 IU/mL continuous saline infusion to prevent the IV system from clots and to reduce the number of catheter-related phlebitis/occlusions [10].

steps.title: Standard protocols
steps.instruction: There are different protocols depending on the hormone to be measured.

steps.title: Standard protocols
steps.instruction: The WHO guidelines on drawing blood recommend inspecting the puncture site and if bleeding occurs then applying gentle pressure on the puncture site until bleeding has stopped. If no bleeding occurred it is recommended to apply a bandage [4].

description: Repeated 24 h blood sampling, a frequently used method in research, is required for time series analyses of metabolites and/or hormones that show strong fluctuations in blood concentration over time. Repeated 24 h blood sampling has a higher failure rate in older adults due to difficulty in establishing and maintaining venous access due to age- induced changes in the integrity of the skin, venous vasculature and valves. Therefore we customized pre-sampling, sampling and post-sampling procedures for continuous sampling in older adults. In our research center, the customized protocol was used for 24 h blood sampling with a sample frequency of 10 min in a group of 41 elderly mean (range) age of 66 (52–76) years. With the customized protocol, the mean (standard deviation) number of missing samples was 6.3 (7.0) out of 144 (4.4%). Thus, the customized protocol that is discussed in more detail below represents a useful and successful method for high frequency sampling of blood in healthy older adults.
